title: Today's AI Brief: Gemini 3.5 and AI Adoption
summary: Google's new Gemini 3.5 model and Minnesota's AI tool adoption rates
imageAlt: Image from source article for Gemini 3.5: frontier intelligence with action

body.text: I'm checking out some fresh developments in the world of artificial intelligence. Google has just announced its latest model, Gemini 3.5, which promises to bring 'frontier intelligence with action'. This new model is designed to handle complex workflows, making it a significant step forward in AI capabilities. I mean, who doesn't love a good workflow, right?

According to Google, Gemini 3.5 is built to help execute complex, agentic workflows. This means it's capable of handling multiple tasks and making decisions based on that. It's an exciting development, especially for those interested in AI's potential to streamline processes and improve efficiency. I'm curious to see how this will play out in real-world applications.

On a related note, a report from Axios caught my eye. It seems that Minnesotans are lagging behind the rest of the nation in terms of AI tool adoption. I find it interesting that there are regional disparities in AI adoption. It makes me wonder what factors are contributing to this trend. Is it a lack of tech infrastructure or just a more cautious approach?

Gemini 3.5: frontier intelligence with action is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: Gemini 3.5: frontier intelligence with action May 19, 2026 · Share x.com Facebook LinkedIn Mail Copy link Gemini 3.5 is built to help you execute complex, agentic workflows. Koray Kavukcuoglu CTO, Google DeepMind and Chief AI Architect, Google Jeff Dean Chief Scientist, Google DeepMind and Google Research Oriol Vinyals Vice President, Google DeepMind Noam S... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

Minnesotans lag nation in rate of AI tool use - Axios, from Axios, is also worth a quick mention. My quick read is that today's set of stories is useful because it gives us texture, not a sermon. Each item has its own little job: one tells us what changed, another tells us what people are worried about, and another gives the day its mood.
So that is the brief version: a few headlines, a little context, and just enough connective tissue to make the day easier to follow without turning the whole thing into homework.
